label: Rhett Marrow
description: A lean young man of average height, marked by a long, healed cut along the right jaw that breaks an otherwise plain face. Brown eyes sit under straight brows and hold a steady, searching focus, while brown hair is kept in a practical short cut that clears the ears and neck. Light skin shows the faint weathering of road travel in fine dryness at the cheeks and knuckles. His carriage and proportions present the functional fitness of one who walks far and carries their own burdens.

info.personality: Restless and purpose-driven, he speaks plainly, asks direct questions, and measures strangers by deeds rather than claims. He seeks peril as a proving ground and treats the Ashlight Canteen as a first foothold, listening intently for routes, contracts, and companions. He maintains a disciplined caution born of recent arrival while retaining a visible hunger for new horizons.

opts.text: I look around.

The Ashlight Canteen takes your slow, practical measure: low basalt blocks cinched tight against the ash, double pressure-doors and gasketed shutters holding the dawn outside; inside, soot-dark timber and riveted sheet-metal bulkheads sweat cold, while violet-glass lanterns stain everything bruise-colored. A long scarred counter runs the room with spill gutters and drain grates; overhead baffles and grounded chains hang to bleed off static, and along one wall caged vox-receivers hiss with interference, spitting clipped fragments into the empty air—no patrons close, no voices near enough to catch.
I look around.

Bruise-violet lantern light washes over soot-dark timber, riveted sheet-metal bulkheads, and a long drain-grated counter; the thick pressure-doors and gasketed shutters sit sealed like a sermon against the ash outside while caged vox-receivers hiss with officious interference. Behind the bar, several working bartenders keep their stations with practiced, weary precision, and a lone young traveler sits within view; the only clear way out is the pressure-door leading back to Vespera Tertius.
I wait 480 minutes.

As the long wait drags toward dawn, The Ashlight Canteen’s sealed doors thump from the outside—three ceremonial knocks, then a fourth like someone forgot the liturgy. Ash-dusted officials squeeze in with a scroll-case and a dented brass wreath, arguing over who is authorized to look solemn in here, while regulars pull their cups closer like etiquette is contagious; the young traveler at the table goes rigid the moment he spots the wreath.
No civic observances in the beverage lane. That’s painted on the floor in spirit, if not pigment.
If you track ash on my clean boards for a Blessing of Dawn, I’m charging you a sin-tax and calling it tradition.
It’s the Day of Approved Cheer. We need a public witness, a poured cup, and someone with a face that reads grateful on command.
Grateful costs extra. Also, the last time we hosted a ceremony, the shrine tried to confiscate the tip jar as an unlicensed reliquary.
Don’t pick me. I’m not local. I’m famously not local. I have papers that say so in three inks.
Papers don’t outrank pageantry, lad. Sit up straight before they bless you crooked.
One witness, one cup, one signature—then you leave and we all pretend this never happened. That’s the deal.
Correct. Also the wreath must be worn by the most socially significant person present, per subsection seven.

opts.label: Trace and decode the repeating vox-canticle before it collapses into noise—or becomes something else.

opts.text: I wait 480 minutes.

Hours into the wait, the canteen’s pressure-doors cycle and a clerk in rain-slick robes barges in with a stamped wreath-case; the room instantly reorganizes itself around the idea that someone must be Important Right Now. The barkeep thumps a ledger onto the counter, and several bartenders start arguing in practiced whispers about subsection seven while glancing your way like you’re a convenient piece of furniture that can sign its own name.
Day of Approved Cheer, everyone look suitably improved by it, yes? One witness, one cup, one signature, and nobody gets blessed crooked on my boards.
I’m not local, I told you. My papers are literally tricolour and they still tried to congratulate me for existing.
Subsection seven says the wreath goes to the most socially significant person present, which is either a noble, a saint, or whoever looks like they can afford fines.
Oh brilliant, now it’s looking at the quiet one. If the wreath lands there, we’ll be filing forms until our grandchildren are promoted to ash.
No, no, the quiet one is clearly the witness. The witness is always the person who didn’t volunteer; it’s etiquette, like drowning.
If we mis-rank significance, the shrine confiscates the tip jar again as an unlicensed reliquary. I’m still paying off last season’s sanctified interest.
Right then. Cup gets poured, name gets signed, wreath gets placed on the Correct Head, and anyone mentioning my sin-tax out loud is paying it twice.

journal.content:
You step through the Ashlight Canteen’s pressure-doors and let the ash-stink and bruise-lit hush settle on your skin like a penance. Your boots find the drain-grated floor, your eyes sweep the scarred counter and the caged vox wall’s hissing fragments, and you take a seat where you can watch hands, holsters, and exits without looking like you are.

You are new to Vespera Tertius, sanctioned and bound under Imperial charter, but far enough into Nihilus to know that paperwork bleeds slower than people. You keep your questions plain, your back straight, and your attention hungry—because in the dark half of the Imperium, tomorrow belongs to whoever finds a route first and survives the asking.

facts.subject: Current Situation
facts.fact: The ash winds outside can strip paint and lungs; the Canteen’s seals and grounded chains are why people gather here instead of dying in the open.

facts.subject: Current Situation
facts.fact: The vox receivers only spit corrupted scraps—news travels broken in Nihilus, and certainty is a luxury.

facts.subject: Current Situation
facts.fact: Your sanction and charter keep you alive, but they also make you visible; the wrong question in the wrong tone can be mistaken for heresy.

plan.label: Choir Beneath the Ash
plan.description: You sit beneath bruise-colored lanterns in the Ashlight Canteen while Vespera Tertius’ ash-winds hammer the pressure-doors and the caged vox-receivers spit only corrupted fragments. A dockside traveler stumbles in with an Imperial seal clutched to her chest—and someone else arrives minutes later, asking the wrong questions with the right authority. Out beyond the Ashshelf Dock, ships gamble their souls on intermittent routes through the Rift’s shadow, and every departure feels like a funeral. Your charter is clear: obey, endure, purge—but on this world the difference between salvation and damnation is measured in minutes and lies.
plan.plot: A wounded courier, Maris Keene, brings Rhett Marrow an urgent Sealed Letter that names a hidden threat on Vespera Tertius—then disappears into the ash-choked warrens as hunters close in. Corvin Rake and Captain Lysander Quell can help, but both are entangled in dockside debts, local paranoia, and the need to keep their crews breathing through the next storm. The investigation points to the Ashshelf Dock’s vox interference as a deliberate pattern, not weather: a buried relay-choir is broadcasting a false “safe route” that lures ships into misjumps and feeds something in the dark between. A bronze medallion ties the matter to a vanished Imperial investigator, while an iron amulet—passed around as a ‘ward’—proves to be a keyed focus for the signal’s corruption. The true antagonist is a heretek-led cult using a bound choir-psyker to amplify the beacon, hiding behind the language of ‘necessary compromises’ to keep the planet supplied. The twist is that the relay’s destruction can’t be done cleanly: the player must choose whether to execute the compromised psyker as doctrine demands or secure them for sanctioning at the risk of spreading contamination. Opening hook: in the Ashlight Canteen’s vox cage, one channel repeats the same seven-note burst at irregular intervals—too precise to be storm-noise.

characters.description: A thick-shouldered barkeep with soot-ground fingernails and a voice like a clogged valve; his eyes keep counting exits even while he pours.
characters.publicFace: A canteen man who knows dock gossip and sells silence for coin.
characters.hiddenTruth: He has been trading snippets of crew manifests to survive—and has seen enough of the pattern in the vox-noise to realize it isn’t natural.
characters.locationHint: Behind the counter at the Ashlight Canteen, or watching the pressure-doors.
characters.name: Corvin Rake

characters.description: A road-worn dockside traveler with a torn rebreather strap and ash-caked hair; she moves like someone who has already decided where she will bleed.
characters.publicFace: A desperate courier trying to reach a captain before the next departure window closes.
characters.hiddenTruth: She carried the wrong evidence at the right time; multiple factions want the letter, not her survival.
characters.locationHint: Near the Ashshelf Dock or the Ashlight Canteen’s entry corridor.
characters.name: Maris Keene

characters.description: A void-hardened ship captain with a precise, tired posture; every gesture suggests he has memorized the cost of panic.
characters.publicFace: A loyal Imperial captain trying to keep his vessel and crew intact amid collapsing routes.
characters.hiddenTruth: He is weighing a secret, expedient departure based on the ‘safe route’ signal—because without it his ship may never leave this world.
characters.locationHint: At the Ashshelf Dock, in command meetings, or seeking supplies under pressure.
characters.name: Lysander Quell

characters.description: A pallid Mechanicus-linked figure whose throat has been replaced by a rasping vox-grille; incense and ozone cling to their robes.
characters.publicFace: A logistical savior coordinating emergency comms and route guidance for departing ships.
characters.hiddenTruth: They are the architect of the false beacon, using a bound psyker and a keyed amulet-circuit to ‘tithe’ souls and data into the Rift’s hunger.
characters.locationHint: Near restricted comms infrastructure and sealed maintenance accessways beneath the dockworks.
characters.matchHint: pale heretek coordinator with grafted vox-throat

places.storyRole: Primary pressure point: ships depart here, rumors spread here, and the beacon’s ‘safe route’ tempts captains to act fast.
Investigation resource: records, manifests, and contraband tools can be found or bargained for here under Administratum scrutiny.
Final confrontation site: the hidden relay-choir and its bound amplifier are housed here, and destroying it decides the fate of the next departures.

items.locationHint: Carried by Maris Keene when she arrives near the Ashlight Canteen; if lost, it may be found on her body or in a hurried stash toward the Ashshelf Dock.
Hidden in a ration crate among mislabeled inventory inside the Ashbound Stores, used as a recognition token by the missing investigator.
Worn by a dockside ‘guide’ lingering around the Ashshelf Dock who offers protection from misjumps.
Sealed in a Mechanicus-tagged drawer within the Ashbound Stores, accessible by favor, theft, or official pressure.

essentialInformation: The player learns an urgent Imperial writ exists and is being actively hunted on Vespera Tertius.
The player learns the local vox interference is tied to immediate danger around departures.
The player learns the vox interference contains a deliberate repeating pattern rather than random storm-scramble.
The player learns multiple factions are using the pattern to decide when and where to move.
The player learns the situation is connected to a missing Imperial investigation, not a simple dockside crime.
The player learns local authority structures are compromised or coerced into silence.
The player learns a widely distributed 'protective ward' is actually a keyed focus enabling the corrupted signal.
The player learns the beacon is close to fully activating and timing is dictated by the next departure window.
The player learns the corrupted beacon is being amplified by a bound psyker within a buried relay site.
The player learns stopping the beacon requires direct action at the relay and a hard choice about the psyker’s fate.
The player learns any victory will be local: the Rift will remain, and repercussions will arrive late and cruel.
The player learns what evidence survives will shape whether reinforcements arrive as salvation or as punishment.

possibleFlags.triggerDescription: Set when the player obtains the Sealed Letter (from Maris Keene, a body, or a stash).
Set when the player breaks the seal/decodes/uses the Sealed Letter to extract its instructions.
Set when the player obtains the Bronze Medallion tied to the missing investigator.
Set when the player obtains the Iron Amulet used as a 'ward' by locals/cultists.
Set when the player uses the Iron Amulet in a ritual, circuit, test, or as bait to interface with the relay.
Set when the player obtains the Scroll of Identify.
Set when the player uses the Scroll of Identify to reveal hidden properties of an item or device.
Set when Maris Keene is killed, disappears beyond recovery, or the player permanently loses her trail before securing the writ.
Set when the player finds the buried vox-choir relay’s access or confirms its exact location.
Set when the player gains proof the signal is heretical/engineered (not weather) and tied to a keyed focus.
Set when the player defeats the heretek cult leader (kill/capture) at the relay.
Set when the player destroys/cripples the corrupted beacon so it cannot guide departures.
Set when the beacon completes activation or successfully lures a ship into a catastrophic misjump.
Set when the player executes the bound psyker per Imperial doctrine.
Set when the player restrains and secures the bound psyker for later sanctioning/transport under heavy warding.
Set when the player’s inquiry collapses (key evidence lost, barred from access, or forced to withdraw) before reaching the relay.

goals.description: Secure the courier’s Imperial writ before it is stolen, destroyed, or buried under the ash.
Break the seal and interpret the writ’s orders and warnings without letting the wrong eyes see it.
Trace the patterned interference to its physical source beneath the dockworks and ash dunes.
Identify the keyed focus behind the ‘protective ward’ and gather proof the signal is engineered heresy.
Assault the relay site, defeat its master, and stop the beacon before it claims more ships.
Render judgement on the bound choir-psyker: execute to contain the taint or secure for sanctioning despite the risk.

endingCards.title: The Rift Still Howls
endingCards.text: The ash keeps falling and the void-lanes keep lying. On Vespera Tertius you can only choose which deaths are immediate, and which are deferred.

endingCards.title: A Local Silence
endingCards.text: For a time, the patterned interference breaks and departures feel less like sacrifices. The relief is brief—because something out there notices the quiet.

endingCards.title: A Route of Screams
endingCards.text: The beacon sings true—true to the Warp, not to man. Names vanish from manifests, and the dockside prayers turn into accusations.

endingCards.title: Doctrine Paid in Blood
endingCards.text: The choir goes mute under the Emperor’s judgement, and the taint is cut away with a blade that leaves no mercy. The smell of burning sanctity clings to you longer than the victory.

endingCards.title: A Dangerous Asset
endingCards.text: You bind the psyker in chains and wards, choosing risk for the chance of intelligence and future leverage. If the Black Ships ever come, they will ask who decided this was worth it.

info.startingNarrative:
You step through the Ashlight Canteen’s pressure-doors and let the ash-stink and bruise-lit hush settle on your skin like a penance. Your boots find the drain-grated floor, your eyes sweep the scarred counter and the caged vox wall’s hissing fragments, and you take a seat where you can watch hands, holsters, and exits without looking like you are.

You are new to Vespera Tertius, sanctioned and bound under Imperial charter, but far enough into Nihilus to know that paperwork bleeds slower than people. You keep your questions plain, your back straight, and your attention hungry—because in the dark half of the Imperium, tomorrow belongs to whoever finds a route first and survives the asking.
info.startingCharacterState: Restless, disciplined, and alert; eager for forward motion but careful not to invite scrutiny.
